key_insight: Swift heavy ion impacts in Al2O3 create a radially nonuniform band-gap collapse, with metallic behavior near the track core and partial gap shrinkage farther out, which strongly changes how energy is transferred to the lattice.
engineering_relevance: Track-damage models for irradiated oxides should account for spatially varying electronic structure, because ignoring radial band-gap collapse can underpredict discontinuous damage formation along ion paths.
method: TREKIS Monte Carlo simulates the initial electronic and ionic excitation from the ion passage.
DFT-based molecular dynamics tracks time-dependent band-structure changes in the ion track.
The resulting radial band-gap profile is used to estimate local atomic heating and damage.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that a 700 MeV Bi ion causes metallization within about 2 nm of the trajectory, a 3–5 nm band-gap shrinkage zone, and little effect beyond 5 nm.
watch_item: The conclusions rely on a coupled simulation workflow, so accuracy may depend on how well the electronic-excitation and DFT-MD models represent real irradiation conditions.
paper_title: Effect of radially heterogeneous band gap collapse on formation of swift heavy ion tracks in Al2O3
